final_payload.description: To satisfy the layer goal, cookieValue(name) must be called with a non-null cookie name that exists in the parsed cookie list produced from the first "Cookie" header in this.headers. The global sink requirement requires that Cookie header string to contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attributes (e.g., different $Path) so that the downstream CookieDecoder mis-parses attributes and later computes an integer overflow in getMaxAge() (returning Integer.MIN_VALUE). Setting this.headers to contain a first "Cookie" entry whose value includes two cookies with the same name ("SID") and differing $Path plus an oversized Max-Age forces inconsistent attribute association during decoding; then calling cookieValue("SID") will find the first matching HttpCookie named "SID" in cookies() and return its value, ensuring the malicious cookie header is actually processed in order to build the cookie list that triggers the vulnerable parsing path.

field_values.cookies(): InboundCookieParser.parse(headers("Cookie")) returns a non-null List<HttpCookie> containing at least one HttpCookie with name == "SID"; parsing the Max-Age=2147483648 tokens may overflow decoder logic so getMaxAge() can become Integer.MIN_VALUE

final_payload.description: The wrapper method org.webbitserver.wrapper.HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue(String name) is a thin delegate that forwards the non-null name argument to the underlying StubHttpRequest.cookieValue(name). In StubHttpRequest.cookieValue, it resolves an HttpCookie by scanning cookies parsed from the request's "Cookie" header(s) via InboundCookieParser.parse, then returns cookie.getValue(). To satisfy the sink-level constraints and exercise the vulnerable cookie decoding logic, this.request must be a non-null StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains at least one entry with key "Cookie" and a single cookie header string that repeats the same cookie name multiple times and injects conflicting attribute-like tokens (e.g., "$Path" and "Max-Age") so the decoder mis-associates these tokens across duplicates. The concrete header below repeats the cookie name "SID" twice, and includes multiple "$Path" and "Max-Age" tokens interleaved between duplicates; under the described buggy decoder, this can cause attribute parsing inconsistencies leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() (ending at Integer.MIN_VALUE). The method under analysis will then find at least one HttpCookie with name == "SID" and return its value, ensuring the vulnerable parsing path is executed during cookies() / cookie(name).

final_payload.description: To satisfy this layer’s preconditions, httpClient and logger must be non-null so the method can execute through both the success and warning branches without NullPointerException. The exploit-relevant input is the HttpUriRequest’s URI: it must have a user-info component that includes additional '@' characters so that downstream URI authority parsing (e.g., via URIUtils.extractHost in later call-chain layers) can misinterpret the authority/host-port boundary and yield a null/incorrect HttpHost. In this method, the crafted URI is used when executing the request and when logging request.getURI() on non-200 responses. A concrete malicious URI example is one where user-info contains an extra '@' before the real host, such as 'http://user@attacker@victim.com:80/path'. This creates ambiguous authority parsing because multiple '@' appear in the user-info/authority section; parsers that split on '@' incorrectly may treat parts of the host as user-info or vice versa, triggering the described vulnerability in subsequent host extraction logic. The context can be null (it does not affect the path here), and charset is assumed valid (UTF-8).

trace.selected_text_preview: HttpResponse response = httpClient.execute(request, context);

final_payload.description: In this main method, the program ignores args entirely and instead hardcodes the URL and calls sign(WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket(), url). Therefore args[0] cannot influence WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket() or any downstream FastJSON deserialization sink described by the Global sink-level constraints. To satisfy the layer template preconditions (non-null array, at least one element), we provide a benign single-element array. No additional fields are referenced or can be determined at this layer. If the intended vulnerable sink is inside WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket() and requires attacker-controlled JSON (with an '@type' gadget), that control is not reachable from main() as written; additional context for getJsapiTicket() (its parameters/inputs or global configuration it reads) is required to craft a concrete malicious arg0 that actually triggers the sink.

final_payload.description: InstallTemplate.run() takes no parameters and only calls execute() inside a try/catch. To satisfy the layer goal, we must set the instance field 'folder' so that folder.get(0) is a single malicious string. The chosen value is a Windows UNC path (starts with \\) followed by directory traversal segments (..\\) which is a known pattern that can cause Apache Commons IO FilenameUtils.normalize(...) to return null (due to invalid/unsafe normalization of UNC + traversal). When later code (inside execute()) relies on normalize() to validate/canonicalize the path, the null result can bypass checks or be mishandled, enabling path traversal behavior. Independently, if an IOException is thrown, the catch block will print the exact malicious folder[0] value, confirming propagation of the payload.

trace.selected_text_preview: System.out.println("Unable to install template: " + folder.get(0));

final_payload.description: The wrapper method org.webbitserver.wrapper.HttpRequestWrapper.cookies() simply delegates to this.request.cookies(). In the StubHttpRequest implementation, cookies() parses the raw Cookie header via InboundCookieParser.parse(headers("Cookie")). To satisfy the layer goal, this.request must be a org.webbitserver.stub.StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains at least one entry with key "Cookie" and a non-null value. The provided Cookie header string includes multiple cookie definitions with the same cookie name ("SID") and conflicting attribute assignments (duplicate $Path plus Max-Age set to 2147483648, which overflows a 32-bit int). This kind of inconsistent/duplicate attribute parsing can cause the decoder to mis-associate attributes across duplicates and trigger integer overflow in getMaxAge(), yielding Integer.MIN_VALUE, meeting the global sink-level constraint.
